summary: My name is Bob L. Blackburn Junior, born September 10th, 1951, in Oklahoma City. Around history circles, I'm known as Doctor Bob. I've been deeply involved in preserving Oklahoma's history, and I often say the Oklahoma History Center is the house that Bob built. My work has inspired others to record stories and memories that help us understand our times better.

I was born in Oklahoma City, and much of my life and work has centered here. My mother worked in television here, notably at KOCO-TV, and I’ve spent a lot of time preserving the city's history through the Oklahoma Historical Society. Oklahoma City is where my family settled, and it’s been the backdrop for many important events in my life and career.

We are recording this interview at the Oklahoma History Center in Oklahoma City, which serves as the flagship headquarters of the Oklahoma Historical Society. I often say this is the house that Bob built, reflecting my deep involvement in its creation and mission. The center holds exhibits, including one about cotton picking that connects to my family history.

The Oklahoma Historical Society is the organization behind the Oklahoma History Center, where we preserve and share the state's history. I’ve worked closely with them, contributing oral histories and exhibits that document Oklahoma’s past. Their mission aligns with my passion for recording voices and stories that might otherwise be lost.

My dad's family has long roots in Claremore, Oklahoma, where he was born in 1920. Though I was born in Oklahoma City, Claremore represents my paternal heritage and the place where my family's story began in the state. It’s a significant part of my family background.

My mother's side of the family comes from Western Oklahoma, with connections to places like Chickasha and Ninnekah. This region shaped much of her upbringing and influenced her personality and career. It’s part of the broader Oklahoma landscape that I grew up hearing about.

Chickasha is part of my mother's background; she was born near there in 1929. It’s one of the small towns in Western Oklahoma connected to my maternal family’s history. These places shaped her early life before she moved to Oklahoma City.

My mother, Ida May Turley, was born in Ninnekah, Oklahoma, in 1929. It was a small rural town where she grew up as the youngest of eight children. Ninnekah represents her early years before she moved into a television career in Oklahoma City.

Norge, Oklahoma, figures in my family history because my grandfather came there to pick cotton in the 1920s. It was the only good cotton year in that decade, and he spent days hauling cotton to the gin near Norge. This story is part of an exhibit at the Oklahoma History Center that connects to my roots.

My grandfather originally came from Howard County, Arkansas, just across the border from Broken Bow, Oklahoma. He moved west to pick cotton near Norge, Oklahoma, in the 1920s, which was a defining moment for our family’s Oklahoma connection. The journey took days in a Model T pickup, showing the hardships they endured.

Broken Bow is near the Arkansas border, close to where my grandfather started his journey to Oklahoma. It’s part of the region connecting my family’s Arkansas origins to their new life in Oklahoma. This area represents the transition from Arkansas to Oklahoma for my maternal ancestors.

My mother, Ida May Turley, was born in 1929 in Ninnekah, Oklahoma, and was the youngest of eight children. She was a talented singer and charismatic woman who broke barriers as a television personality from 1958 to 1975. She hosted Romper Room locally on KOCO-TV and later produced a magazine-format show promoting Oklahoma City, making over 3000 live broadcasts and pioneering as a woman in media and advertising.

Agnes Steelman, known as Aggie, was my mother's oldest sister who had already left home by the time Mom was born. She lived in Duncan, Oklahoma, and was part of the family background that shaped my mother’s early life. Though not much detail is shared, she represents the older generation in my maternal family.

Duncan, Oklahoma, is where my Aunt Agnes Steelman lived after leaving home. It’s part of my mother’s family story and represents the broader rural Oklahoma environment that shaped my maternal relatives. Duncan is one of the small towns connected to my family’s history.

Romper Room was the first television show my mother hosted, starting in the late 1950s on KOCO-TV in Oklahoma City. She was selected after an open audition and trained in Baltimore for a week before returning to host the preschool program locally. It was a one-camera shoot in a small grocery store, and Mom’s role as Miss Ida made her a beloved local figure.

KOCO-TV in Oklahoma City was the station where my mother hosted Romper Room and later her magazine-format show promoting the community. It was a key platform for her television career from the late 1950s through the 1970s. KOCO-TV allowed her to connect with audiences and local businesses, pioneering local programming before syndication was common.

Baltimore is where my mother traveled for a week of training before starting as the local hostess of Romper Room. This trip was crucial in preparing her for the television role she would take on back in Oklahoma City. It represents the early professional development in her media career.

Britton, Oklahoma, was the location of the small grocery store where KOCO-TV produced Romper Room with my mother as the host. It was a practical choice for production, allowing the station to do much of its work outside of Enid. This place is part of the early days of my mother’s television career.

Borden Dairy Company was one of the local businesses my mother promoted on her Romper Room show. She would encourage kids to drink Borden's chocolate milk and even urged her own family to buy it. This was part of her entrepreneurial approach to television, blending education and commercial sponsorship.

Route 66 was significant in my family because my dad was a highway patrolman on that famous highway. We lived in Edmond to stay close to his work, which influenced where my mother could work and live during her television career. Route 66 was part of the backdrop of our family life in Oklahoma.

Dennis Donuts was a local donut shop chain my mother promoted during a year she was out of television. She became their promotion person, traveling to schools in a 1959 Chevy convertible to encourage kids to visit the shops. This work kept her connected to the community and showcased her promotional talents.

Chevrolet figures in our family story because Dennis Donuts bought my mother a 1959 Chevy convertible for her promotional work. It was a black car with a red boot, and she used it to travel around schools promoting the donut shops. This car became a symbol of her entrepreneurial spirit during that time.

Admiral John Kirkpatrick was a legendary Oklahoma City businessman and philanthropist who helped my mother get back on television. He wanted a show that promoted the city and gave free airtime to local cultural institutions. His leadership helped my mother create a magazine-format show that lasted thousands of episodes, supporting the community and local talents.

The Lyric Theatre in Oklahoma City was one of the cultural institutions featured on my mother’s magazine-format television show. The show gave free airtime to places like the Lyric Theatre to promote local arts and fundraisers. This helped connect the community and support the city’s cultural life.

Barton Theatres was another local organization that benefited from my mother’s television show, which provided free airtime to promote events and fundraisers. This helped raise awareness and support for the theaters in Oklahoma City. It was part of the broader community engagement my mother fostered through her work.

Jody Miller, from Blanchard, Oklahoma, was a famous country and western music star whose first public appearance was on my mother’s television show. She was the first woman to win a Grammy in that genre, and her career was launched in part by the exposure she got on the Ida B show. This highlights how my mother’s show helped nurture local talent.

Blanchard, Oklahoma, is the hometown of Jody Miller, a country music star who appeared first on my mother’s television show. This town is part of the network of Oklahoma communities connected through the show’s promotion of local talent. It represents the broader regional impact of the program.

Steve Ripley was a friend and musician who appeared on my mother’s show in the 1960s when he was in a garage band in Oklahoma City. His participation illustrates how the show supported emerging local artists. Steve was someone we both admired, and his early exposure helped his career.

Puerto Vallarta was a place where my mother and her last husband had a home, and she considered retiring there. However, after being widowed, she chose to start an advertising agency in Oklahoma instead of retiring to Puerto Vallarta. It represents a personal and family connection to a place outside Oklahoma.

Bird Dog Rogers was my mother’s fifth husband and a Cherokee Indian. Their marriage lasted longer than her previous ones, and after he passed away, she started the first woman-owned advertising agency in Oklahoma. Bird Dog was a great guy and part of my mother’s later life story.

Bird Dog Rogers, my mother’s last husband, was a Cherokee Indian, connecting our family to the Cherokee people. This heritage is part of the broader cultural fabric of Oklahoma and adds to our family’s diverse background. It also influenced my mother’s later life and work.

The Children’s Center in Oklahoma City was one of the causes my mother supported through her advertising agency. She even wrote a jingle for them and gave free advertising time to help promote their work. This reflects her commitment to community service and using her talents for good causes.
